---
title: "Product and quotient spaces"
description: "Product spaces, quotient spaces, affine subsets and combinations, the quotient map, and the first isomorphism theorem — an augmented summary of Axler's LADR §3.E."
date: "2026-06-06"
categories: ["mathematics", "linear-algebra", "ladr"]
execute:
    echo: true
---

{{< include ../../_macros.qmd >}}

This is an augmented summary of Section 3.E of [Linear Algebra Done Right](https://linear.axler.net/index.html) by Sheldon Axler.

## Product of vector spaces
The **product space** $V_1\times\cdots\times V_n$ of vector spaces $V_1,\cdots,V_n$ (all over $\mb{F}$) is their Cartesian product. (This shouldn't be confused with the product of linear maps.)

If we define addition and scalar multiplication on $V_1\times\cdots\times V_n$ as element-wise operations, we can show that the product space is a vector space over $\mb{F}$.

#### Dimension of product
Suppose that the $V_i$s are finite-dimensional. Then $V_1\times\cdots\times V_n$ is also finite-dimensional, with its dimension given by the sum of the individual dimensions of the $V_i$s.

:::{.proof}
Let $v_{1,1},\cdots,v_{1,k_1}$ be a basis of $V_1$. Then the vectors

$$(v_{1,1},0,\cdots,0),\cdots,(v_{1,\dim V_1},0,\cdots,0)$$

are linearly independent and span $V_1\times0\times\cdots\times0$. Similarly, the vectors

$$(0,v_{2,1},0,\cdots,0),\cdots,(0,v_{2,\dim V_2},0,\cdots,0)$$

are linearly independent and span $0\times V_2\times0\times\cdots\times0$. The combined set of vectors are still linearly independent, and span $V_1\times V_2\times0\times\cdots\times0$. 

Continuing in this manner, we see that a total of $\dim V_1+\cdots+\dim V_n$ linearly independent vectors span $V_1\times\cdots\times V_n$. Therefore, we have

$$\dim(V_1\times\cdots\times V_n)=\dim V_1+\cdots+\dim V_n.$$

:::

Conversely, if $V_1\times\cdots\times V_n$ is finite-dimensional, we can show that the component spaces are finite-dimensional.

:::{.proof}
If $V_1\times\cdots\times V_n$ is finite-dimensional, then there exist vectors

$$(v_1^1,\cdots,v_n^1),\cdots,(v_1^m,\cdots,v_n^m)$$

that form a basis of this product space. But this implies that the set $v_i^1,\cdots,v_i^m\in V_i$ spans $V_i$ for each $i$, so the $V_i$s are finite-dimensional.
:::

#### Product to sum
Let $U_1,\cdots,U_n$ be subspaces of $V$, and consider the linear map 

$$\Gamma:U_1\times\cdots\times U_n\to U_1+\cdots+U_n$$

that is defined as

$$\Gamma(u_1,\cdots,u_n)=u_1+\cdots+u_n.$$

By definition, $\Gamma$ is surjective.

###### Conditions for directness
In the setup above, $U_1+\cdots+U_n$ is a direct sum if and only if $\Gamma$ is injective (and thereby invertible).

:::{.proof}
$U_1+\cdots+U_n$ is a direct sum.

$\iff$ $u_1+\cdots+u_n=0$ if and only if $u_1=\cdots=u_n=0$.

$\iff$ $\ker\Gamma=\{0\}$.

$\iff$ $\Gamma$ is injective.
:::

Furthermore, $\Gamma$ is injective if and only if

$$\dim(U_1+\cdots+U_n)=\dim U_1+\cdots+\dim U_n.$$

That is, a subspace is direct if and only if the dimension of the sum is the sum of the individual dimensions.

:::{.proof}
$\Gamma$ is injective.

$\iff$ $\ker\Gamma=\{0\}$.

$\iff$ $\dim(U_1\times\cdots\times U_n)=\dim(U_1+\cdots+U_n)$ by the rank-nullity theorem applied to $\Gamma$, using the fact that $\Gamma$ is surjective.

We have seen that 

$$\dim(U_1\times\cdots\times U_n)=\dim U_1+\cdots+\dim U_n.$$

:::

#### Splitting linear map of product
The vector space $\mathcal{L}(V_1\times\cdots\times V_n,W)$ is isomorphic to $\mathcal{L}(V_1,W)\times\cdots\times\mathcal{L}(V_n,W)$.

:::{.proof}
For each $T:V_1\times\cdots\times V_n\to W$, we can associate a series of maps $T_i:V_i\to W$ defined as

$$T_iv=T(0,\cdots,0,v,0,\cdots,0)$$

where $v$ occurs in the $i$th argument of $T$. $T_i$ is linear because $T$ is linear. So consider the map 

$$M:\mathcal{L}(V_1\times\cdots\times V_n,W)\to\mathcal{L}(V_1,W)\times\cdots\times\mathcal{L}(V_n,W)$$

which maps $T$ to $T_1\times\cdots\times T_n$. Since

$$
\begin{aligned}
(S+T)(0,\cdots,0,v,0,\cdots,0)&=S(0,\cdots,0,v,0,\cdots,0)+T(0,\cdots,0,v,0,\cdots,0)\\
&=S_iv+T_iv,
\end{aligned}
$$

we have 

$$M(S+T)=(S_1+T_1)\times\cdots\times(S_n+T_n)$$

and so $M$ is additive. Similarly, we can show that $M$ is homogeneous.

For $M(T)$ to be $0\times\cdots\times0$, $T$ must map each $(0,\cdots,0,v,0,\cdots,0)$ to $0$. If so, the linearity of $T$ leads to

$$
T(v_1,\cdots,v_n)=T(v_1,0,\cdots,0)+\cdots+T(0,\cdots,0,v_n)=0+\cdots+0=0,
$$

which means that $T$ is the zero map. This implies $\ker M=\{0\}$, and so $M$ is injective.

$M$ is surjective because for any set of linear maps $T_i:V_i\to W$, the map

$$T:(v_1,\cdots,v_n)\mapsto T_1v_1+\cdots+T_nv_n$$

is a linear map from $V_1\times\cdots\times V_n$ to $W$. (The linearity of $T$ follows from those of the individual $T_i$s.)

Therefore, $M$ is an isomorphism.
:::

#### Splitting linear map to product
Similarly to above, the vector space $\mathcal{L}(V,W_1\times\cdots\times W_n)$ is isomorphic to $\mathcal{L}(V,W_1)\times\cdots\times\mathcal{L}(V,W_n)$.

:::{.proof}
Define $T_iv$ as the $i$th component of $Tv$, and consider the map

$$M:T\mapsto T_1\times\cdots\times T_n.$$

We can show that $M$ is an isomorphism, by following the steps similar to the previous proof.
:::

#### Isomorphism of vector power
The vector space $V^n$ is isomorphic to $\mathcal{L}(\mb{F}^n,V)$.

:::{.proof}
Let $M:V^n\to\mathcal{L}(\mb{F}^n,V)$ be the map that sends each $(v_1,\cdots,v_n)$ to the function

$$\phi(x_1,\cdots,x_n)=x_1v_1+\cdots+x_nv_n.$$

The linearity of $\phi$ can easily be ascertained. $M$ is additive because

$$
M((v_1,\cdots,v_n)+(u_1,\cdots,u_n))=M(v_1+u_1,\cdots,v_n+u_n),
$$

which is sent to the function

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi(x_1,\cdots,x_n)&=x_1(v_1+u_1)+\cdots+x_n(v_n+u_n)\\
&=(x_1v_1+\cdots+x_nv_n)+(x_1u_1+\cdots+x_nu_n)
\end{aligned}
$$

where the two bracketed terms correspond to $M(v_1,\cdots,v_n)$ and $M(u_1,\cdots,u_n)$ respectively. Similarly, we can find that $M$ is homogeneous, and so $M$ is a linear map.

In order to have $x_1v_1+\cdots+x_nv_n=0$ for all $x_1,\cdots,x_n$, we must have $v_1=\cdots=v_n=0$. It follows that $\ker M=\{0\}$, and so $M$ is injective.

By the linearity of $\phi$, we have

$$\phi(x_1,\cdots,x_n)=x_1\phi(1,0,\cdots,0)+\cdots+x_n\phi(0,\cdots,0,1)=x_1\phi(e_1)+\cdots+x_n\phi(e_n)$$

where $e_i$ is the $i$th standard basis.
If we set $v_i=\phi(e_i)$, we see that $M(v_1,\cdots,v_n)=\phi$, and so $M$ is surjective.

Therefore, $M$ is an isomorphism.
:::

## Quotient space
Let $U$ be a subspace of $V$. Then the **quotient space** $V/U$ is a set of subsets of $V$, given by

$$V/U=\{v+U:v\in V\}.$$

Each subset $v+U$ is called an **affine subset** of $V$ that is **parallel** to $U$.

#### Two affine subsets are equal or disjoint
For any $v,w\in V$ and a subspace $U$ of $V$, the following three statements are equivalent to each other:

1. $v-w\in U$.
2. $(v+U)\cap (w+U)\neq\emptyset$.
3. $v+U= w+U$.

:::{.proof}
Suppose that $v-w\in U$, so that $v-w=u$ for some $u\in U$. Then we have

$$v+0=w+u,$$

which means that this vector is in both $v+U$ and $w+U$ (since $0\in U$). So we find that $(v+U)\cap (w+U)\neq\emptyset$.

Suppose that $(v+U)\cap (w+U)\neq\emptyset$. Then there exist $u_1,u_2\in U$ such that $v+u_1=w+u_2$, from which we have $v=w+(u_2-u_1)$.

For any $v+u\in v+U$, we have

$$v+u=w+(u_2-u_1)+u,$$

which implies that $v+u\in w+U$ and so $v+U\subseteq w+U$. An analogous reasoning shows that $w+U\subseteq v+U$, hence we find $v+U=w+U$.

Suppose that $v+U=w+U$. Then there exist $u_1,u_2$ such that $v+u_1=w+u_2$, which leads to

$$v-w=u_2-u_1\in U.$$

Therefore, we see that (1) implies (2), (2) implies (3), and (3) implies (1). So the three statements are equivalent.
:::

#### Equality of parallel subsets implies equality of subspaces
For any $v,x\in V$ and subspaces $U,W\subseteq V$, if $v+U=x+W$, then we have $U=W$.

:::{.proof}
Suppose that $v+U=x+W$. Then for any $w\in W$, there exists $u\in U$ such that

$$v+u=x+w\implies w=(v-x)+u,$$

so $w\in(v-x)+U$ and $W\subseteq(v-x)+U$. Similarly, for any $u\in U$, there exists $w\in W$ satisfying the same equality, so we have $(v-x)+U\subseteq W$. Therefore, $W=(v-x)+U$.

But for any $v-x\notin U$, we have $0\notin(v-x)+U$, and so $(v-x)+U$ is not a subspace. Since $W$ is a subspace, this implies that $v-x\in U$, and so $W=U$.
:::

#### Intersection of affine subsets
If $A_1$ and $A_2$ are affine subsets of $V$, then $A_1\cap A_2$ is either empty or is another affine subset.

:::{.proof}
Let $A_1=u_1+V_1$ and $A_2=u_2+V_2$, and suppose that their intersection is non-empty with $x\in A_1\cap A_2$. Let $v_1\in V_1$ and $v_2\in V_2$ be such that $x=u_1+v_1=u_2+v_2$. In the absence of other candidate affine subsets, we can conjecture that

$$A_1\cap A_2=x+(V_1\cap V_2).$$

Suppose that $y\in A_1\cap A_2$, with $y=u_1+v'_1=u_2+v'_2$. We need to show that $y\in x+(V_1\cap V_2)$. If we think about two intersecting planes in $\mathbb{R}^3$, we see that $y=x+(y-x)$ with $y-x\in V_1\cap V_2$. This is indeed the case more generally, because

$$
\begin{gather}
y-x=(u_1+v'_1)-(u_1+v_1)=v'_1-v_1\in V_1\\
\text{and}\quad y-x=(u_2+v'_2)-(u_2+v_2)=v'_2-v_2\in V_2. 
\end{gather}
$$

So $y=x+(y-x)$ does show that $y\in x+(V_1\cap V_2)$.

Now suppose that $y\in x+(V_1\cap V_2)$. Then $y=x+v$ for some $v\in V_1\cap V_2$, which implies that $y\in A_1\cap A_2$.

Therefore, $A_1\cap A_2=x+(V_1\cap V_2)$, and so $A_1\cap A_2$ is either empty or is an affine subset.
:::

#### Affine combination
Given vectors $v_1,\cdots,v_n$, its **affine combination** is a special type of linear combination

$$\lambda_1v_1+\cdots+\lambda_nv_n$$

where the coordinates $\lambda_1,\cdots,\lambda_n$ sum to 1.

###### Condition for affine subset
A non-empty subset $A$ of $V$ is an affine subset if and only if, for every pair of vectors in $A$, all their affine combinations are also in $A$. (Note that an affine combination of two vectors can be written as $\lambda v_1+(1-\lambda)v_2$ without restricting $\lambda$.)

:::{.proof}
Suppose that $A=v+U$ for some $v\in V$ and a subspace $U\subseteq V$. For any two $v+u_1,v+u_2\in A$, we have

$$\lambda(v+u_1)+(1-\lambda)(v+u_2)=v+(\lambda u_1+(1-\lambda) u_2)\in v+U$$

as required.

Conversely, suppose that a non-empty subset $A\subseteq V$ is such that

$$\lambda v_1+(1-\lambda)v_2\in A$$

for all $v_1,v_2\in A$ and $\lambda\in\mb{F}$. Let $w$ be a vector in $A$. Then for all $v\in A$, we have

$$\lambda v+(1-\lambda)w=w+\lambda(v-w)\in A.$$

From this, we may conjecture that

$$A=w+\{\lambda(v-w):\lambda\in\mb{F},v\in A\}=w+U,$$

but first we have to verify that $U$ is a subspace. We see that $U$ contains $0$ and is homogeneous by definition. For two vectors $\lambda_1(v_1-w),\lambda_2(v_2-w)\in U$, we have

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_1(v_1-w)+\lambda_2(v_2-w)&=(\lambda_1+\lambda_2)(\frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_1+\lambda_2}v_1+\frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_1+\lambda_2}v_2-w)\\
&=(\lambda_1+\lambda_2)(\frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_1+\lambda_2}v_1+(1-\frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_1+\lambda_2})v_2-w),
\end{aligned}
$$

and so $U$ is additive as well. Therefore, $U$ is a subspace.

Suppose that $x\in A$. Then $x-w\in U$ and so

$$x=w+(x-w)\in w+U,$$

which implies $A\subseteq w+U$. Now suppose that $x\in w+U$. Then there exists $\lambda\in\mb{F}$ and $v\in A$ such that

$$x=w+\lambda(v-w)=\lambda v+(1-\lambda)w.$$

Because $w\in A$ as well, this implies that $x\in A$. So we have $w+U\subseteq A$. 

Therefore, $A$ is equal to the affine subset $w+U$.
:::

###### Affine hull
Given $v_1,\cdots,v_n\in V$, its **affine hull**

$$A=\{\lambda_1v_1+\cdots+\lambda_nv_n:\lambda_1,\cdots,\lambda_n\in\mb{F},\lambda_1+\cdots+\lambda_n=1\}$$

is the smallest affine subset that contains $v_1,\cdots,v_n$.

:::{.proof}
Using the constraint on the $\lambda_i$s to eliminate $\lambda_1$, we see that $A$ consists of vectors

$$(1-\lambda_2-\cdots-\lambda_n)v_1+\lambda_2v_2+\cdots+\lambda_nv_n.$$

This shows that $A$ is the affine subset

$$
A=v_1+\{(-\lambda_2-\cdots-\lambda_n)v_1+\lambda_2v_2+\cdots+\lambda_nv_n:\lambda_2,\cdots,\lambda_n\in\mb{F}\}=v_1+U,
$$

where we may easily check that $U$ is a subspace.

Any affine subspace that contains $v_1,\cdots,v_n$ can be written as $v_1+W$ where $W$ is a subspace containing the vectors

$$v_2-v_1,v_3-v_1,\cdots,v_n-v_1.$$

The smallest subspace that contains these vectors is their span, which is just $U$. So $A$ is the smallest affine subset containing $v_1,\cdots,v_n$.
:::

#### Defining addition and multiplication
We wish to define addition and scalar multiplication on $V/U$ by

$$
\begin{gather}
(v+U)+(w+U)=(v+w)+U\\
\lambda(v+U)=(\lambda v)+U
\end{gather}
$$

respectively. But we have to check that such operations are well-defined.

:::{.proof}
For the addition to be well-defined, we require

$$(v+w)+U=(v'+w')+U$$

for any $v,w,v',w'\in V$ such that $v'-v$ and $w'-w$ are in $U$. For such $v'$ and $w'$, we have

$$(v'+w')-(v+w)=(v'-v)+(w'-w)\in U,$$

and so the required equality holds.

Similarly, we can show that $\lambda v+U=\lambda v'+U$, and so the scalar multiplication is well-defined.
:::

From here, we can show that $V/U$ forms a vector space over $\mb{F}$ under these operations. As $0+U=U$ is the additive identity, $v+U$ is a non-zero vector only when $v\notin U$.

###### Splitting lemma for vector space
If $V/U$ is finite-dimensional, then $V$ is isomorphic to $U\times V/U$.

:::{.proof}
Let $v_1+U,\cdots,v_n+U$ be a basis of $V/U$. Then for every $v\in V$, there exist unique $a_1,\cdots,a_n$ such that

$$
\begin{aligned}
v+U&=a_1(v_1+U)+\cdots+a_n(v_n+U)\\
&=(a_1v_1+\cdots+a_nv_n)+U.
\end{aligned}
$$

Then $v-(a_1v_1+\cdots+a_nv_n)\in U$, so there is (unique) $u\in U$ that equals this sum. By the uniqueness of the $a_i$s and $v$, we see that the map $M:V\to U\times V/U$ given by

$$Mv=(u,(a_1v_1+\cdots+a_nv_n)+U)$$

is well-defined.

If the RHS is $(0,U)$, then we must have $a_1=\cdots=a_n=0$ by the linear independence of the $v_i+U$s. Subsequently, we obtain

$$0=u=v-(a_1v_1+\cdots+a_nv_n)=v-0=v.$$


So $\ker M=\{0\}$ and $M$ is injective. It is easy to check that $M$ is surjective by reconstructing the $v$ required for an arbitrary $(u,w+U)$.

Therefore, $M$ is invertible and $V$ is isomorphic to $U\times V/U$.
:::

###### Finding direct sum from quotient space
Suppose that $V/U$ is finite-dimensional. Then there is subspace $W\subseteq V$ with $\dim W=\dim (V/U)$ such that $V=U\oplus W$.

:::{.proof}
Let $v_1+U,\cdots,v_n+U$ be a basis of $V/U$, where $n=\dim(V/U)$. We will show that the span of $v_1,\cdots,v_n$ is the subspace $W$ that we are seeking.

Suppose that

$$a_1v_1+\cdots+a_nv_n=0.$$

Then this combination is in $U$, so we have

$$
\begin{aligned}
U&=(a_1v_1+\cdots+a_nv_n)+U\\
&=a_1(v_1+U)+\cdots+a_n(v_n+U).
\end{aligned}
$$

So we have $a_1=\cdots=a_n=0$ by the linear independence of the $v_i+U$s, which means that $\text{span}(v_1,\cdots,v_n)$ is a subspace of $V$ with the required dimension. 

For an arbitrary $v\in V$, there exist coordinates $b_i$ such that

$$
\begin{aligned}
v+U&=b_1(v_1+U)+\cdots+b_n(v_n+U)\\
&=(b_1v_1+\cdots+b_nv_n)+U,
\end{aligned}
$$

which means that 

$$v-(b_1v_1+\cdots+b_nv_n)\in U.$$

As $b_1v_1+\cdots+b_nv_n\in W$, this implies that $V=U+W$.

Now suppose that

$$c_1v_1+\cdots+c_nv_n\in U.$$

Then similarly to above, we have

$$U=(c_1v_1+\cdots+c_nv_n)+U$$

and so $c_1=\cdots=c_n=0$. This implies $U\cap W=\{0\}$.

Therefore, $V=U\oplus W$.
:::

## Quotient map
The **quotient map** $\pi:V\to V/U$ is defined by

$$\pi(v)=v+U.$$

It is a linear map because

$$
\begin{gather}
\pi(v+w)=(v+w)+U=(v+U)+(w+U)=\pi(v)+\pi(w)\\
\pi(\lambda v)=\lambda v+U=\lambda(v+U)=\lambda\pi(v).
\end{gather}
$$

#### Constructing basis of $V$ from quotient space
If $v_1+U,\cdots,v_n+U$ is a basis of $V/U$ and $u_1,\cdots,u_m$ is a basis of $U$, then 

$$v_1,\cdots,v_n,u_1,\cdots,u_m$$

is a basis of $V$.

:::{.proof}
Since $\dim V/U=\dim V-\dim U$, we see that the set has the right size to be a basis of $V$.

Suppose that 

$$a_1v_1+\cdots+a_nv_n+b_1u_1+\cdots+b_mu_m=0.$$

Then $a_1v_1+\cdots+a_nv_n\in U$, which implies that

$$(a_1v_1+\cdots+a_nv_n)+U=U$$

and so $a_1=\cdots=a_n=0$. Consequently, the linear independence of $u_1,\cdots,u_m$ implies $b_1=\cdots=b_m=0$. 

So $v_1,\cdots,v_n,u_1,\cdots,u_m$ is a set of linearly independent vectors with the right size, and is therefore a basis.
:::

#### Factorisation into quotient map
Suppose that $U$ is a subspace of $V$ and $T:V\to W$. Let $\pi$ be the quotient map from $V$ to $V/U$.

Then $T$ can be factorised as $T=S\circ \pi$ for some $S:V/U\to W$ if and only if $U\subseteq\ker T$.

:::{.proof}
If there exists such $S$, then for all $u\in U$, we find

$$Tu=S(\pi(u))=S(u+U)=S(0+U)=0.$$

So $U\subseteq \ker T$.

Conversely, suppose that $U\subseteq\ker T$. We will define $S:V/U\to W$ as

$$S(v+U)=Tv.$$

If $v_1+U=v_2+U$, then we have $v_1-v_2\in\ker T$ and so $Tv_1=Tv_2$. Therefore, $S$ is well-defined, and $T=S\circ\pi$ by definition.
:::

###### Pullback
The **pullback** $\Gamma:\mathcal{L}(V/U,W)\to\mathcal{L}(V,W)$ of $S:V/U\to W$ by the quotient map $\pi:V\to V/U$ is the map

$$\Gamma(S)=S\circ\pi.$$

This is an injective linear map whose image is given by the annihilator of $U$.

:::{.proof}
It is easy to check that $\Gamma$ is linear, by the properties of the linear map product.

Suppose that $\Gamma(S)=\Gamma(S')$. This implies

$$
\begin{aligned}
&S(\pi(v))=S'(\pi(v))\text{ for all }v\in V\\
\implies&S(v+U)=S'(v+U)\text{ for all }v+U\in V/U
\end{aligned}
$$

and so $S=S'$. Therefore, $\Gamma$ is injective.

Let $T\in\im\ \Gamma$. Then there exists $S:V/U\to W$ such that $\Gamma(S)=T$. For any $u\in U$, we find

$$
Tu=S(\pi(u))=S(0+U)=0,
$$

and so $T$ is an annihilator of $U$. Now suppose that $T$ is an annihilator of $U$. Then $U\subseteq\ker T$, and we have seen that this implies the existence of $S$ such that $\Gamma(S)=T$. Therefore, we have $\im\ \Gamma=U^0$.
:::

###### Dimension of quotient space
Since the kernel of $\pi$ is $U$ and $\pi$ is surjective, the rank-nullity theorem gives us

$$\dim V/U=\dim\im\ \pi=\dim V-\dim\ker\pi=\dim V-\dim U.$$

## First isomorphism theorem
Given a linear map $T:V\to W$, we can define $\tilde{T}:V/\ker T\to W$ as

$$\tilde{T}(v+\ker T)=Tv.$$

We will show that $\tilde{T}$ is an isomorphism between $V/\ker T$ and $\im\ T$, in steps.

First, we need to show that $\tilde{T}$ is a well-defined linear map.

:::{.proof}
If $v+\ker T=u+\ker T$, then $v-u\in\ker T$. This implies that $Tv=Tu$, so $\tilde{T}$ is well-defined.

The linearlity of $\tilde{T}$ follows from our definitions of addition and multiplication on $V/U$.
:::

We can then show that $\tilde{T}$ is injective and surjective (when we take the codomain to be $\im\ T$).

:::{.proof}
Suppose that $\tilde{T}(v+\ker T)=\tilde{T}(w+\ker T)$. This implies $Tv=Tw$, and so $v-w\in\ker T$. Therefore, $v+\ker T=w+\ker T$ and $\tilde{T}$ is injective.

By the definition of $\tilde{T}$, we see that the images of $\tilde{T}$ and $T$ coincide. So $\tilde{T}$ is surjective on $\im\ T$. 
:::

Hence, $V/\ker T$ is isomorphic to $\im\ T$. This is called the **first isomorphism theorem**.

:::{#exm-}
For a non-zero linear functional $\phi\in\mathcal{L}(V,\mb{F})$, we have $\dim\im\ \phi=\dim\mb{F}=1$. So the first isomorphism theorem gives us

$$\dim (V/\ker\phi)=1.$$

:::
